data.title: Expand Energy explores sale of most of iconic Oklahoma campus, say broker, documents
data.description:
Key points:
  • 100 acre plus campus includes data center, restaurants, fitness centre
  • Company plans to retain headquarters, few other buildings, broker says
  • Campus part of Chesapeake Energy founder Aubrey McClendon's legacy

Expand Energy , the largest U.S. natural gas producer formerly known as Chesapeake, is looking to sell the bulk of its storied Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, campus, according to a document reviewed by Reuters and the brokers involved in the planned sale.

The planned sale of the real estate marks another step in the shrinking of the iconic Chesapeake brand, once synonymous with the U.S. shale revolution.

The company has hired real estate brokers at Colliers International Group and Cushman & Wakefield's Commercial Oklahoma division to market over 100 acres (40.5 hectares) of the campus in the northern suburbs of Oklahoma City.

Of the 26 buildings on the campus, Expand plans to retain its 253,000-square-foot (23,505-sq-meter) headquarters known as Building 15, three large buildings, four garden-style buildings, a parking garage and about two acres of land, said Travis Mason, director at Cushman & Wakefield's Commercial Oklahoma division.

Sale considerations for the rest of the real estate, which includes a 56,250-square-foot data center with capacity for 2.9 megawatts of critical power generation, are at an early state, Mason noted.

The sale is likely to attract interest from real estate developers, while other companies looking to buy individual buildings could also be in the mix, said Walker Ryan, a principal at Colliers.

Mason and Ryan refused to provide a valuation for the real estate, and said it would depend on whether the properties are sold altogether or in different transactions.

Expand did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

The company, which in 2020 fell into bankruptcy, last year changed its name as part of a $7 billion merger with rival Southwestern Energy. The merger made it the largest U.S. natural gas producer, but its headcount has dropped drastically in recent years following scores of layoffs.

The campus, once a status symbol for the company and Oklahoma City, was meticulously planned by Chesapeake's late founder Aubrey McClendon as he grew the company from 10 employees when he co-founded it in 1989 to over 12,000 in 2012.

McClendon, who died in a single-car crash in 2016, spared little expense to build the campus, which includes a 67,000-square-foot fitness center with an Olympic-sized swimming pool, four restaurants and a 62,000-square-foot daycare facility.

At its peak early in the previous decade, over 8,000 of Chesapeake employees worked at the campus, according to The Oklahoman. As of last year, that number had shrunk to 560 employees, the newspaper reported.

Chesapeake's total workforce stood at about 1,000 employees by the end of 2023, according to annual filings.

data.title: CoStar Targets Australia's Domain With $1.68B Takeover Proposal — Update
data.description:
By Stuart Condie

SYDNEY--U.S. real-estate advertiser and analytics provider CoStar has proposed a $1.68 billion takeover of Domain, Australia's second-largest property advertiser.

Domain on Friday said that CoStar, which operates U.S. portals including Homes.com, made an indicative cash proposal worth 4.20 Australian dollars, equivalent to about $2.66. That represents a near 35% premium to Domain's share price before the local share-market open.

Domain said its board would assess the proposal. CoStar has already acquired an almost 17% stake at the proposed offer price, Domain said.

CoStar's proposal comes 14 months after it completed a $125 million takeover of OnTheMarket, one of the U.K.'s three most-visited residential property portals. At the time, CoStar CEO Andy Florence said his company would integrate OnTheMarket into its network and invest millions of dollars in the U.K. business.

CoStar and Nine Entertainment, the Australian media conglomerate that owns 60% of Domain's shares, didn't immediately respond to requests for comment.

The sale by Nine of its controlling interest would present an opportunity for significant capital management, E&P analyst Entcho Raykovski said in a note.

Last week, Domain reported a 46% jump in December-half profit. Its shares are up 23% this year at A$3.12, but its market capitalization is dwarfed by that of News Corp-controlled rival REA Group.

Domain has long labored unsuccessfully to overcome REA's first-mover advantage in Australian online property classifieds. REA, still the No. 1 player, has a market capitalization of US$22.53 billion.

News Corp. owns Dow Jones & Co., publisher of this newswire and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Select Medical Q4 Adjusted Earnings, Revenue Increase; 2025 Outlook Issued -- Shares Drop After Hours
data.description:
Select Medical Holdings reported Q4 adjusted earnings late Thursday of $0.18 per share, up from $0.12 a year earlier.

Four analysts polled by FactSet expected $0.17.

Revenue for the quarter ended Dec. 31 was $1.31 billion, up from $1.22 billion a year earlier.

Three analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $1.28 billion.

The company said its 2025 earnings will likely be $1.09 to $1.19 per share. Analysts polled by FactSet expect $1.08.

The company now expects full-year revenue of $5.4 billion to $5.6 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect $5.37 billion.

Shares of Select Medical traded 9% lower in after-hours activity.

data.title: Allison Transmission Approves $1 Billion Additional Share Repurchases; Raises Dividend 8%
data.description:
Allison Transmission said Thursday its board approved a new $1 billion share repurchase program.

The new authorization brings the company's total repurchase threshold to $5 billion.

The company also raised quarterly dividend 8% to $0.27 per share, payable March 14 to shareholders of record on March 3.
